content:
12 min read
1 hour ago

--

Async code rarely fails because it looks complicated. It fails because timing, ownership, cancellation, and failure paths are easier to ignore than they look.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Senior engineers do not read async code by asking whether it runs.
They read it by asking what can happen while it is waiting.
That difference matters more than most developers realize.
Async/await made asynchronous code easier to read. It made promises look cleaner. It removed callback pyramids. It made network calls, file operations, timers, jobs, and background workflows feel almost sequential.
But async/await did not make asynchronous behavior simple.
That is where many bugs hide.
The code looks calm in review. A function starts. It awaits a request. It updates state. It saves something. It returns a result. Everything reads from top to bottom like normal code.
Real systems do not behave that politely.
Requests overlap. Users click twice. A page changes before a response returns. A worker retries a job. A network request succeeds after the user no longer cares. A save fails halfway. A loading flag turns false while another operation is still running. An old response overwrites a newer one. An error gets caught too far from the source and loses its meaning.
The syntax looks sequential.
The system is still asynchronous.
Junior developers often ask, “Does this async code work?”
Senior engineers ask, “What happens if it runs twice, finishes late, fails halfway, or no longer matters by the time it completes?”
That is the real difference.

Async/Await Makes Waiting Look Harmless
The dangerous thing about async/await is that it makes waiting look normal.
A function like this feels easy to trust:
async function loadProfile(userId: string) {
setLoading(true);
const profile = await fetchProfile(userId);
setProfile(profile);
setLoading(false);
}There is nothing obviously scary here. Start loading. Fetch the profile. Set the profile. Stop loading.
That is why the bug survives review.
The code only describes the happy path. It does not describe what happens if the user changes accounts before the request returns. It does not describe what happens if fetchProfile fails. It does not describe what happens if loadProfile is called twice. It does not describe whether this response is still relevant by the time it arrives.
Async bugs often live in the distance between starting work and finishing it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A senior engineer does not only read the lines that exist. They read the time between the lines. They ask what can change while the function is suspended at await.
Can the input change? Can the user leave? Can another request start? Can the component disappear? Can the job be retried? Can the external service partially succeed? Can the permission change? Can the cache be invalidated?
Most async code works when nothing changes during the wait.
Production is where things change during the wait.
That is the first thing experienced engineers notice. The code may be clean, but the waiting period has no protection.
Sequential Code Can Still Create Race Conditions
A lot of async code looks safe because each line happens after the previous one.
But sequential syntax does not prevent overlapping executions.
Imagine a user typing into a search box. Each change triggers a request. The request for “rea” starts first. The request for “react” starts second. The second request returns quickly, so the UI shows results for “react.” Then the older request returns late and overwrites the screen with results for “rea.”
Every individual function call worked.
The system still showed the wrong result.
That is a race condition most developers do not notice until latency exposes it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The bad assumption is subtle: developers assume the last request started will also be the last request that matters. Real networks do not promise that. A slower old request can finish after a faster new request. If the code accepts every response equally, the UI eventually lies.
A simple version looks like this:
async function search(query: string) {
const results = await searchUsers(query);
setResults(results);
}This code does not ask whether the response still belongs to the current query. It only asks whether the request succeeded.
That is not enough.
A better version makes relevance explicit:
let latestSearchId = 0;
async function search(query: string) {
const searchId = ++latestSearchId;
const results = await searchUsers(query);
if (searchId !== latestSearchId) {
return;
}
setResults(results);
}This is not about this exact pattern. Some systems use cancellation. Some use query libraries. Some use request keys. Some use server-side search sessions. The mechanism matters less than the rule.
Old work should not be allowed to update new reality.
Senior engineers notice when async code has no relevance check. They know the code may pass local testing because local timing is too friendly. The bug appears later when latency, quick user actions, retries, or background refreshes create a different order than the developer imagined.
Async code is not only about what happens next.
It is about what else can happen before next arrives.
Loading States Often Lie
Loading state looks simple until more than one async operation can exist at the same time.
Many developers write loading state as a boolean:
setLoading(true);
await saveSettings();
setLoading(false);That works when there is exactly one operation and it always completes cleanly.
But real interfaces rarely stay that simple.
A user can click save twice. A page can load data while another request refreshes permissions. A dashboard can fetch five widgets at once. A form can validate a field while submitting the full payload. One request can fail while another succeeds. A background refetch can start while the visible action is still pending.
Now isLoading becomes suspicious.
Which operation is loading? The initial page data? The save button? The permissions check? The export request? The background refresh? The retry?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A single boolean can flatten different kinds of waiting into one vague state. The UI may enable a button too early because one request finished while another still matters. Or it may keep a spinner visible because a background request is running even though the user-facing operation is done.
This is one of the small things senior engineers notice quickly. They look at loading state and ask whether it represents reality or only a convenient mood.
Bad loading state creates bad user behavior. Users click again because the button looks available. They navigate away because the screen looks stuck. They submit a form twice because the disabled state was tied to the wrong request. They see “saved” while a related update has not actually completed.
The better approach is to name the waiting.
Initial loading is not the same as saving. Saving is not the same as validating. Validating is not the same as refreshing. Refreshing is not the same as retrying.
This does not mean every async operation needs a complex state machine. Overengineering loading state can become its own mess. But important async work should be represented clearly enough that the UI does not lie about what is happening.
A senior engineer does not trust isLoading by default.
They ask, “Loading what?”
Missing Cancellation Turns Old Work Into Future Bugs
Cancellation is one of the easiest async concerns to ignore because the happy path does not need it.
The request starts. The request finishes. The code updates the screen.
Everything looks fine.
But what happens when the work no longer matters?
The user navigates away. The selected account changes. The modal closes. The component unmounts. The background job is replaced by a newer job. The input query changes. The user cancels the action. The system starts a newer request with better information.
If the old async work continues and writes its result later, it can corrupt the current state.
This is how stale data appears after navigation. This is how a closed modal updates a form that no longer exists. This is how an old account’s data briefly flashes on a new account page. This is how an outdated permission check disables the wrong action.
Cancellation is not only about saving network bandwidth.
It is about ownership.
Who owns this async work now? Is the caller still alive? Is the result still relevant? Is there a newer operation that replaced it? Is this response still allowed to change anything?
In browser code, cancellation may use AbortController. In backend jobs, cancellation may mean checking job status before continuing. In queues, it may mean idempotency keys or deduplication. In services, it may mean timeouts and context propagation. In UI workflows, it may mean ignoring results from work that is no longer current.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The exact tool changes by environment.
The principle does not.
Async work should not assume it is still welcome when it finishes.
Senior engineers look for that assumption. They notice when async code starts work but never defines what makes the work obsolete. They know that in real systems, not all completed work should be applied.
Sometimes finishing late is worse than failing.
Failure Paths Are Usually Too Optimistic
Async code often hides weak failure handling because the happy path reads so nicely.
async function submitOrder(order: OrderInput) {
const payment = await createPayment(order);
const savedOrder = await saveOrder(order, payment.id);
await sendConfirmation(savedOrder.id);
return savedOrder;
}This looks readable. It also raises uncomfortable questions.
What happens if payment succeeds but saving the order fails? What happens if the order saves but the confirmation email fails? What happens if the request times out but the payment provider actually charged the customer? What happens if the user retries? What happens if the backend receives the same operation twice?
Most developers read this as a sequence.
Senior engineers read it as a list of partial failure states.
That difference is critical.
Async systems do not always fail cleanly. They can fail after doing something important. They can time out after the external service completed the operation. They can retry and duplicate side effects. They can leave the system between states that no UI was designed to show.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The problem is not that every async function needs heavy transaction logic. The problem is pretending failure only happens before anything important occurs.
Some operations need compensation. Some need idempotency. Some need retries. Some should not retry automatically. Some need a status model that can represent “accepted,” “processing,” “completed,” “failed,” and “unknown.” Some need logs that explain where the sequence stopped.
A senior engineer will ask, “What state is the system in if this line succeeds and the next one fails?”
That question exposes many weak async designs.
The answer cannot always be “we show an error.”
An error message does not undo a payment. It does not unsend an email. It does not remove a duplicate job. It does not repair a partially updated database record. It only tells the user something went wrong.
Failure handling is not only communication.
It is system design.
Retries Can Duplicate Side Effects
Retrying feels safe when the operation is read-only.
If a request to fetch data fails, trying again is usually reasonable. There are still concerns, but the risk is smaller.
Retries become dangerous when the operation changes something.
Create a payment. Send an email. Submit an order. Process a refund. Create an invoice. Reserve inventory. Publish a message. Start a background job.
If this kind of operation retries without protection, the system may do the same real-world action twice.
